question.text: Scenario: Karan Singh is advising a manufacturing company that exports automotive components. The following economic changes have occurred simultaneously: (1) RBI has cut the repo rate by 75 basis points over the last 6 months from 6.50% to 5.75%. (2) The Indian Rupee has depreciated 8% against the US Dollar from Rs.82 to Rs.88.50. (3) Global crude oil prices have fallen 25% from $90 to $67.50 per barrel. (4) India's GDP growth has improved from 5.5% to 7.0%. (5) The US Federal Reserve has started cutting rates, weakening the US Dollar globally. (6) WPI inflation has fallen from 6.5% to 3.2%. The company earns 60% of its revenue from exports (invoiced in USD) and imports 30% of its raw materials (also priced in USD). The remaining revenue is domestic, and 70% of raw materials are sourced domestically. Karan needs to assess the net impact on the company. Question 4 of 5: The combined effect of all six economic changes on this company is:

answer.text: Mixed — some factors help and others hurt, but the net effect is significantly positive
No impact as positive and negative factors cancel out exactly
Positive for revenue but negative for profitability

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Rate cut: lower borrowing cost (positive). INR depreciation: higher export revenue (positive). Lower crude: lower costs (positive). GDP growth: domestic demand up (positive). Falling WPI: lower input costs (positive). US rate cuts: favorable for EM assets. Almost all factors favor this company. **The Trap:** While 30% imported raw materials become costlier due to INR depreciation, this is more than offset by the 60% export revenue boost, lower crude/energy costs, and improved domestic demand. **Cross-Exam Context:** Multi-factor economic analysis tested in NISM Series XV and X-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This is why economic analysis matters for stock selection — a company that benefits from multiple macro tailwinds can outperform significantly.
